turns.content: I'm trying to understand the difference between a simple destructor call and using an RAII guard pattern in my code.

The main distinction is that calling drop() manually on a local variable forces its execution immediately at that point, whereas an RAII guard like a MutexGuard holds ownership of the resource until it goes out of scope. When you wrap a resource in a struct implementing Drop, the destructor runs automatically when that specific instance is dropped, ensuring cleanup happens exactly when needed without manual intervention.
Okay so if I have a variable holding a file handle and I call drop() on it right now versus wrapping it in a Guard struct, does the timing of release actually matter?

Yes, timing matters significantly because Rust's garbage collector isn't involved; destructors run deterministically based on stack unwinding. If you hold onto a Guard instance longer than necessary, the resource stays locked or held open longer than intended. Conversely, if you call drop() prematurely without releasing all dependencies correctly, you might leave the system in an inconsistent state where other parts of your code expect the resource to still be available.
That sounds dangerous, but I think I misunderstood something earlier when reading about this. Can you clarify what happens if I try to access a field after its Drop trait implementation has run?

Once the Drop trait runs for a struct, that specific instance is considered destroyed and cannot be accessed again; any attempt to read or write fields afterward will panic at runtime because the memory has been reclaimed. The key insight here is that you must ensure all necessary cleanup completes before moving on to code that assumes the resource still exists.
